title: Reference Points Spillovers: Micro-Level Evidence from Real Estate
abstract: Homeowners who originally bought when marketwide price levels were high (low) fetch high (low) sales prices and rents, even decades later. We study the propagation of reference-dependence toneighboringlistings. The “spillover” reference point effect is about one-half as large as the “own” reference point effect. Neither house quality nor location appears capable of explaining the result. Using a simple model to provide empirical predictions, we find support for a competition-based mechanism. We quantify the aggregate effect of own and spillover reference point effects on aggregate prices and/or rents at the ZIP code level.

author.Marco Giacoletti*: Marshall School of Business, University of Southern California,USA
author.Christopher A Parsons: Marshall School of Business, University of Southern California,USA

A growing body of research explores how reference points affect individual decision-making and financial markets. However, both the manner in which reference points are established, and how they affect behavior vary. For example, loss aversion relies on a utility “kink” around the reference point. In real estate, loss aversion—with the reference point often being a seller’s original purchase price—appears to be particularly significant for predicting both prices and volume.1Alternatives have been developed based on liquidity constraints and/or down payment requirements. In these cases, reference points (e.g., the purchase price) affect seller behavior not directly through the utility function, but by the discontinuous impact they impose on the seller’s other choices, such as moving to another home.2A final strand of the literature focuses on the impact of personal experiences to establish reference points, which in turn distort expectations relative to a rational, full-information benchmark.3

This paper explores the possibility of reference point “spillovers” from one real estate investor to another via price competition. Two reasons make the housing market an attractive setting for studying this issue. First, because of the large ebbs and flows that have characterized many U.S. housing markets, heterogeneity in thetimingof purchases creates substantialcross-sectionalvariation in reference points. For example, in Los Angeles, the same house would have sold for about 40% less in 2009 compared to 2006 and would have then appreciated well over 100% from 2009 to 2019. Second, although differing over time and by location, active house listings usually do not face perfect substitutes, giving sellers a degree of market power over potential buyers. Because the prices of competing (e.g., nearby) houses are linked through competition, the impact of one seller’s idiosyncratic reference point may exert an influence extending beyond her own house, hence giving rise to spillovers.

We estimate reference point spillovers using two data sets on residential housing. The first involves sales of single-family residences in California. This data set is constructed using information from Corelogic and consists of roughly 230,000 sales occurring between 2017 and 2018, where we observe the date in which properties were originally purchased by their current owners.4The second contains data on about 30,000 rental houses, also in California, from December 2018 through March 2019. Although we are mostly interested in the larger data set on sales, previous research has shown the effect of reference points, induced by historical purchase prices, on landlords’ rent setting (Giacoletti and Parsons 2022), thus affording some measure of external validity.5

To establish the reference point for a given homeowner, we use the value of the Zillow price index in the ZIP code where the house is located, at the time of original purchase. On average, these prior sales occurred 9–10 years prior to the current sales or listing event. The advantage of using this historical ZIP code index approach—compared to using individual house purchase prices—to establish reference points is that the latter may reflect persistent, unobserved quality characteristics, which may in turn be reflected in resales prices. Fluctuations in ZIP code indexes, on the other hand, reflect variation in aggregate pricing, which have little to do with the idiosyncratic quality of individual houses.

Figure 1shows the intuition behind our empirical tests. For a given ZIP code, suppose that housesA(shown in green) andB(red-orange) are both put up for sale or listed for rent, in 2018. Immediately proximate to both houses are several comparable houses either actively on the market or that have sold recently. The respective purchase dates of these competitors are represented as little crosses (A’s competitors) and squares (B’s competitors), with the same color as the original house; house A’s competitors are green, and house B’s competitors are red-orange. In the figure, house A competes with houses that were acquired when aggregate prices were relatively low, whereas house B’s competitors entered the market when prices were relatively high. Our key identifying assumption is that, because of sampling variation in the cross-section of houses (AandB), the historical timing during which the portfolio of competing houses were originally acquired is as-good-as-random. If so, in a cross-sectional regression, we can interpret the average value of price indexes at time-of-purchase for each house’s competitors as exerting a causal influence on its own pricing.

Fig. 1 Illustrative example of the empirical strategy The figure illustrates an example of the identification strategy used in our paper. In the background, we plot the S&P Case-Shiller Index for the city of Los Angeles, available from the St. Louis Fed database ( https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/LXXRSA ). We then consider two houses for sale (or rent), A and B, in 2018. Assume the two houses were originally bought at the same point in time. We overlay on the time series of the house price index the time of last purchase of the neighboring houses for sale (or rent) for house A (green crosses) and house B (red-orange squares). To identify spillover effects, we exploit differences in the timing of purchase of neighboring comparables, after controlling for the own purchase timing, house characteristics, and location. We estimate spillover effects with both reduced-form and two-stage estimates. For the former, we regress the sales price of a given house on (1) the Zillow price index prevailing when it was purchased (i.e., the own effect) and (2) the same quantity, but averaged across nearby, competing listings (i.e., the spillover effect). We also control for a wide array of house attributes, as well as location-by-time fixed effects. In the two-stage approach, we estimate the residual pricing effect on a house’s comparable listings—comparing hedonic models that, respectively, include and exclude the historical Zillow price index as a covariate—and feed the average residual into the main pricing equation, to obtain a “pass-through” coefficient.

The empirical estimates suggest that indeed, the price that house sellers (or rent that landlords) can obtain depends on not only their own acquisition timing but also that of their neighbors. The selling price elasticity with respect to the own ZIP code price index at the time of purchase is roughly 0.025, while the corresponding variable for neighboring sellers within a half-mile is about 0.011 (t= 2.76). A similar ratio is found in the two-stage estimates, which indicates that about 50% of the extra-hedonic pricing residual of one’s competitors is reflected in a house’s own closing price. Comparable coefficients in the rental market give similar, though larger estimates, both for the own and spillover effects. In both the sales and rental markets, expanding the “comparable radius” gradually diminishes the coefficient, indicating a highly localized competitive effect.

When interpreting the results, an important concern is that the timing of historical purchases across comparable listings might be capturing individual house or micro-location characteristics which, if true, would lead to a spurious relation between pricing and historical price indexes. Several pieces of evidence cut against this alternative. First, exploiting a longer horizon for sales, we show that contemporaneous competition is crucial for generating spillovers. More specifically, while the historical conditions of a house’s active and/or very recent competitors—having closed less than 30 days ago—affect prices, those of would-be competitors, but having sold only a few weeks prior (31-90 days) do not. If historical conditions proxied for micro-locational attributes, we would expect to observe a similar impact for both groups.

As a further test of omitted locational heterogeneity, we directly test whether houses in a given “acquisition vintage,” for example, having been bought in 2006 when aggregate prices were high, are clustered geographically with a ZIP code. Within each ZIP code, we calculate the distribution of pairwise distances between all pairs of houses treated with “high” surrounding reference points, identified by having competing listings with an average historical Zillow value over the median for the ZIP code. We then compare this distribution to that for houses treated with “low” surrounding reference points and find that the distributions are statistically indistinguishable. Because neither group exhibits excess clustering, relative to each other or to the overall ZIP code population, the historical conditions of one’s peers are unlikely to reflect unobserved locational attributes.

With these basic findings established, the second part of the paper explores the economic mechanism driving reference points spillovers. Our intuition is based on the idea that with standard Bertrand competition, the price chosen by one seller (or landlord) will influence those optimally chosen by others. Thus, if a seller’s (or landlord’s) reference point influences the valuation of her asset, this will not only affect the price of her asset but also affect those of competing sellers (or landlords).

Formalizing this intuition, we propose a stylized model involving two competing home sellers (or landlords) and one potential buyer (or tenant). The value of each house is characterized by the same distribution, but with independent realizations observed only by the buyer. Each seller posts a take-it-or-leave-it price, to which the buyer compares the value of the house. The buyer then purchases whichever house offers the higher surplus, provided it exceeds his reservation utility (zero). In the benchmark case, both sellers believe (correctly) that their house values are drawn from the same distribution, so they set the same price.

The more interesting case is when one of the sellers (or landlords)—because of a reference point that depends on prevailing market conditions at the time he purchased the house—has a distorted view of the house’s value, and so sets a price that is either too high or too low relative to the benchmark with no reference point. In this case, the other seller, who is not similarly “treated” with a reference point, nevertheless sets prices as though she is “treated.” More specifically, if the first seller sets a price that is too high, the second seller will also move her price upward, though to a lesser degree. Intuitively, this is because the second seller now faces a less competitive environment, since the surplus offered by the first seller is smaller, and thus less likely to exceed the surplus offered by the unbiased seller. Consequently, the second seller raises her price, in the limit approaching the monopolistic (i.e., single house) case as the size of the bias increases. Likewise, if the first seller sets a price that is too low, and consequently, results in a higher expected surplus for the buyer, the rational seller also lowers her price to some extent, to the buyer’s further benefit.

The model offers two testable empirical predictions. First, imperfect competition is critical since, as the number of competing sellers increases, the magnitude of the spillover effects converges to zero. To test this prediction, we expand our regression model by including interactions between the spillover variable and the number of comparable listings. We find that the interaction coefficient is negative, and highly significant, in both the sales and rental market. This is consistent with higher competition among sellers attenuating spillovers.

Second, competitive spillovers affect both sales prices and sales probabilities. In particular, for the properties “receiving” the spillovers, the more positive (negative) the impact of the reference point on competitor pricing, the higher (lower) is the probability of sale. This occurs because when a seller’s reference dependence causes him to “overprice” his house relative to hedonic attributes, neighboring houses become more attractive to buyers, even after accounting for these neighboring sellers having shaded their price upward. The data confirm this prediction. For a given property, when the average ZIP code index at purchase among comparable listings is higher, the probability of selling within various horizons (30, 45, 60 and 90 days) is also higher. The magnitude of the effect is nonnegligible: a 50% higher index among comparable listings translates into a 3%–4% (relative) higher sales probability for each horizon.

In our final exercise, we estimate the aggregate effects induced by reference points in both the sales and rental markets. More specifically, we ask whether the collective own and spillover effects of bust-purchased houses contribute significantly to differences in house prices and rents across ZIP codes. To answer this question, we use our micro-estimates of the direct and spillover effects of historical purchase prices, along with the distribution of ZIP code indexes at the time of purchase within ZIP codes, and generate ZIP code-level estimates of reference point effects. In the sales market, we find that the effects of reference points across ZIP codes are on average negative, with mean equal to –0.76%. This is consistent with the fact that in 2017–2018 house prices in California were close to an all-time peak. Roughly a quarter of this combined effect is driven by spillovers. Moreover, variation across ZIP codes is substantial, with an interquartile range for the combined effect between –1.4% and –0.4%. We find effects of similar magnitude in the rental market.

Our findings are related to the growing literature on the transmission of individual biases. Rather than through an information channel, as inHirshleifer (2020)orDeMarzo, Vayanos, and Zwiebel (2003), in our setting, biases transmit via competition. Our work is also related to theoretical contributions that study the competitive interactions between biased and unbiased agents (Cutler, Poterba, and Summers 1990;Scheinkman and Xiong 2003;Barberis, Greenwood, and Shleifer 2015). Finally, our results provide evidence of a novel driver of local spillovers in the housing market. While evidence of spillovers is already present in the literature, existing contributions mainly focus on the negative price effects experienced by properties neighboring foreclosures (Campbell, Giglio, and Pathak 2011;Anenberg and Kung 2014), which are typically concentrated in market downturns. We show that houses purchased at abnormally high, or low prices generate spillovers on neighboring properties even after decades, and that the historical purchase timing of current resales and rentals drives wedges in prices and rents across ZIP codes.
We construct a data set of house sales and rental listings covering the state of California. We obtain data from Corelogic on repeated sales for the same house through deed and tax assessment information. We focus on single-family residences (SFRs) sold in 2017 and 2018 in California, which were last purchased between 1980 and 2018. We exclude houses purchased through short sales and REOs. The data set includes a total of 231,642 house sales.

Panel A ofTable 1reports the summary statistics. The average sales price is approximately $647,000, with a median of $460,000. The average (median) size is 1,799 (1,602) square feet. Roughly 48% of properties have three bedrooms and 46.5% have two bathrooms. The average property age is 44.5 years. The average of the most recent purchase price is $524,500, while the median is $343,000.

We also collect data on rentals from a major online rental listings service. The data were collected from December 2018 through March 2019. We also restrict the sample of rentals to single-family residences. We exclude houses for which the number of bedrooms, the number of bathrooms, size or information on the last sale date are not available. We further exclude houses last purchased before 1980, and houses that have construction dates more recent than their last purchase dates. Finally, to eliminate zombie listings, we exclude properties that have been listed on the website for more than 300 days at the time of collection.

The final data set consists of 30,274 rental listings. Panel B ofTable 1shows summary statistics for the sample of rentals. At the time the data were collected, listings had been online for an average (median) of 39 (23) days. The average monthly rent in the dataset is about $3,800, with a median of $2,850. The distribution of size, last purchase prices, age, and number of bedrooms and bathrooms are similar between the sales and rental data sets.

Figure B.1in theInternet Appendixshows the distribution of house sales and rental listings across California ZIP codes. The data set provides widespread coverage of the state. The spatial distribution of sales and rentals is similar, and more concentrated in main urban areas.
article.2 Reference Point Spillovers:
Prior research has indicated that historical prices, going back even a decade or more, continue to exert an influence on selling and/or renting strategies. Several mechanisms have been proposed. Some directly rely on preferences/utility (e.g., loss aversion),6others operate through liquidity or financial frictions (e.g., downpayment constraints),7and yet another group operates through non-Bayesian expectations formed by personal experiences.8Although reference points are conventionally discussed in the concept of prospect theory, we use the term more broadly in this paper, to refer to the impact of historical signals that, despite not reflecting information about fundamentals, nevertheless affect pricing behavior.

2.1 Reduced-form estimates

We are primarily interested in the spillover of one seller’s historically established reference point to others. Hence, we estimate the following regression: γ δ log ( p i ) = γ log ( p i , last , ZIP 0.5 m l ) + δ log ( p i , last , ZIP ) + B X i + a zip + a y m + e i , (1) where p i is either the current sales price or current listed rent, and X i is a vector of house characteristics, including dummies for number of bedrooms and bathrooms, building age, age squared, the logarithm of the building size, and indicators for other characteristics (e.g., availability of parking spaces or garages). For rental properties, the vector contains also information on appliances, laundry availability and pet policy. a zip are ZIP code fixed effects, and a ym are year-month fixed effects. 9

The impact of the reference point variables is captured by parametersδandγ. The first provides an estimate of the seller/landlord’sownhistorically-derived reference point(s) on later sales or rental transactions. Coefficientδis estimated usingpi,last,ZIP⁠, the level of the local ZIP code price index, which we measure using the Zillow Home Value Index10(ZHVI), at the time of selleri’s most recent (i.e., “last”) purchase. Recall fromTable 1, for both sales and rentals, the typical listing was last purchased around a decade prior. Hence, to the extent thatpi,last,ZIPis related to contemporary transactions, it suggests a long-lived impact of real estate cycles on later prices.

A number of prior papers have provided estimates ofδ, either using the historical purchase price itself or an index similar to ZHVI. Although the pricing effect of a seller’s own reference point is not our primary interest,Internet AppendixA confirms both sets of findings for the sales and rental markets. Columns 1 and 2 ofTables A.1and A.2 suggest that prior sales prices remain strongly related to subsequent sales and rental terms, whereas columns 3 and 4 show a similar effect for the historical Zillow price index. Although the magnitudes are considerably (2-5 times) larger when using actual past sales prices as explanatory variables, our preferred specification relies on fluctuations in the ZHVI index. In this case, we are approximating the ideal case when two identical houses, A and B, sell in different years—say in 2000 and 2006, when prices had more than doubled in California—so thatδcaptures only the “pure” pricing effects related to historical transactions, and not unobserved heterogeneity in house or location quality.

Our main interest is coefficientγ, which provides an estimate of reference point spillovers from a seller or landlord’s neighbors. The key variable ispi,last,ZIP0.5ml⁠, distinguished from the above by the superscript “0.5 ml.” This is meant to indicate that the historical ZHVI is averaged over the competing sales or rental listings for housei, within a half-mile radius. More specifically, within this perimeter, we identify all sales occurring within the last 30 days that (1) have square footage within 1,000 square feet and (2) differ at most by one bedroom, in either direction. The same criteria in points (1) and (2) apply to the rental market, but here we identify comparable listings as those which are listed at the same time.

On average, in the sales market, two houses fulfill these criteria, with slightly more for the rental market (2.5). Among the corresponding portfolio of these comparable listings, we calculate the average of ZHVI, and use this as an explanatory variable in our price and rental regressions. Critically, we also includepi,last,ZIP⁠, the ZHVI for the seller/landlord’s own historical transaction, meaning thatγis estimated by comparing two sellers facing different peer sets (i.e., competitors having bought at different times), but controlling for their own entry point in the real estate cycle.

Table 2reports our estimates ofEquation (1)for house sales. The estimates forγshow a positive, significant impact of comparable listings transactions histories on prices, indicating the spillover of reference points. The estimated elasticity implied byγis around one percent, roughly half the effect of a seller’s own history (δ). Progressing across the table, neither the inclusion of selleri’s own historical experience (column 2), nor the logarithm of the number of comparable listings (⁠log(Ni0.5ml)shown in column 3) has a meaningful effect on the estimated magnitude of the spillover. Both comparisons foreshadow later analysis, where we consider more explicitly the possibility that variation inpi,last,ZIP0.5mlcaptures the competitive environment facing selleri, or other geographic factors acting as potential confounds. Here, we simply note that while a seller’s own history appears relevant for pricing, as does the number of neighboring listings—note the negative sign reflecting a tilt in the competitive balance favoring potential buyers—it appears disadvantageous to be competing with sellers having purchased during times when prices were low (say 2010), and vice versa.

The comparable results for the rental market are shown inTable 3. As with sales, the effect of a landlord’s own history on rent is about twice the effect of the spillover, although both effects are substantially larger in the rental market. One possible reason is that compared to sales, the rental market is characterized by less professional expertise, both on the landlord and tenant sides. In recent years, nationwide, less than 10% of sellers have forgone using a realtor,11whereas, even on online listings websites, up to half of landlords (Giacoletti and Parsons 2022) list their houses without an agent. That both the seller’s own reference points, as well as those of competitors, exhibit a larger pricing effect in a market where brokers are less prevalent helps further with interpretation.

Specifically, if the realization ofpi,last,ZIP0.5mlis regarded (even erroneously) as reflecting fundamentals, we would not expect professional advice to attenuate the relation between historical purchase timing and prices. With the caveat that the sales-rental comparison involves more than just the prominence of real estate professionals, the markedly larger effects among rentals are generally what we would expect if historical conditions establish uninformative reference points, influencing a seller’s or landlord’s own pricing directly (e.g., through loss aversion or downpayment effects), and the prices of nearby listings via competitive response.

The localized nature of residential real estate markets implies that any spillover effects should weaken with distance. To test this, for each house, we start by constructing concentric annuli outside the 0.5-mile radius. The closest ring has an outer diameter ofXmiles, and an inner diameter ofYmiles. We construct rings forX/Ypairs of 1.5/0.5 (ring 1), 2/1.5 (ring 2), and 5/2 (ring 3). For each ring, we randomly select a number of listings equal to the number of comparables located within a 0.5-mile radius. We then construct 100 random samples, and calculate the placebo spillover variable as the average across draws of the mean level of the ZIP code price index for comparables (⁠p¯last,ZIP0.5−Xml⁠). Our results for both the sales and rental market are reported inTable B.1in theInternet Appendix. For all rings, the estimated coefficients for the placebo spillover variables are not statistically significant, with point estimates close to zero.

2.2 Two-stage estimates

The results thus far represent reduced-form estimates, with the historical price index entering directly as a regressor in price and rent regressions. An alternative is a two-stage approach. For house i , define the set of comparable listings as indexed by j = 1 , 2 , …. J ⁠ . For each comparable j , we estimate two hedonic models. The first includes all the controls in Equation (1) , but excludes the historical ZIP code index prices at time of purchase. The second model is identical, except it includes the historical index at the time house j was originally purchased. Finally, we calculate the difference between the forecasts produced by the two models. Formally, for each comparable j , we calculate log ( p ) ^ 1 , j = B ^ 1 X j + a ^ 1 , zip + a ^ 1 , y m , log ( p ) ^ 2 , j = β ^ log ( p j , last , ZIP ) + B ^ 2 X j + a ^ 2 , zip + a ^ 2 , y m , e ^ j = log ( p ) ^ 2 , j − log ( p ) ^ 1 , j .

Then, for each house i —the house whose price we are ultimately interested in explaining—we calculate the average fitted pricing error as: e ^ i , last , ZIP 0.5 m l = ∑ j ∈ J ( i ) e ^ j N J ( i ) ⁠ , where J ( i ) is the set of comparable listings for resale i , and N J ( i ) is the number of comparables. This average fitted pricing error can be interpreted as the average relative pricing error across comparables, explained by the historical level of house prices (for comparables) at the time of purchase. In the final step, we estimate the following regression equation: ϕ δ log ( p i ) = ϕ e ^ i , last , ZIP 0.5 m l + δ log ( p i , last , ZIP ) + B X i + a zip + a y m + e i . (2)

Coefficientϕϕthen can be interpreted as the fraction of “mispricing” traceable to the historical conditions of housei’s comparable listings, which is passed through to its own sales price.12We report estimates ofEquation (2)in columns 4, 5, and 6 ofTable 2. Estimates ofϕϕare statistically significant, and close to 0.5.13This is consistent with the reduced-form estimates ofEquation (1), which also indicate a ratio of approximately 2-to-1 between the own and spillover reference point effects. The last three columns ofTable 3repeat the analysis for rentals. Here, the pass through coefficient is around 0.7, a value that is somewhat higher than that for sales, but one that is still similar to what the reduced-form estimates imply.
article.3 Do Reference Points Reflect Locational:
When interpreting the results above, an important concern is that the apparent impact of historically derived reference points on prices—directly for sellers and landlords, and indirectly through spillovers to neighbors—may reflect unobserved heterogeneity in locational attributes, rather than exerting a causal impact on prices. Two subcategories emerge from within this family of alternatives: what we call “fixed” location effects and “induced” location effects.

In the first,pi,last,ZIP0.5mlcould be correlated with existing, but unobserved (to us) features of housei’s immediate location. This could occur, for example, if more houses in desirable parts of a ZIP code—having parks, less traffic, better schools, etc.—most recently sold when aggregate prices were high (e.g., 2004–2006). Such geographic-specific patterns in turnover could induce spurious correlation between the cross-sectional realizations of historical price indexes and current prices, but with neither reference points, let alone their spillovers to competing listings, playing a role.

A second “induced” effect is more subtle, involving aqualityspillover, but not apricespillover. This is essentially the same issue tackled inCampbell, Giglio, and Pathak (2011)andAnenberg and Kung (2014), where the authors attempt to disentangle the mechanism involved with spillovers of foreclosure sales on nearby listings. Although foreclosures usually sell for steep discounts due to institutional factors (i.e., prices may be low, holding quality constant), their neglect or even abandonment may exert an additional negative impact on the values of neighboring properties. In theory, a similar effect could operate in our context, although the average time between consecutive purchases is roughly a decade. Hence, a plausible story in this vein would require (1) houses most recently purchased when prices were high (e.g., 2005–2007) being disproportionately associated with renovations or property improvements, (2) such investments having positive spillovers to neighboring houses (e.g., landscaping), and (3) the effects being highly durable, lasting a decade on average.

An alternative, but related, channel could be that houses surrounded by comparables that were purchased at times when prices were higher are themselves more likely to be remodeled. This could be because of owners wanting to match their comparables better quality and recent upgrades, or because, even just in the month or months preceding sale, sellers seeing higher surrounding sales prices are more likely to upgrade their house before resale.

In this section, we present the results of four tests intended to make headway on one or both alternatives:

Section 3.1 shows that neither houses with high nor houses with low realizations of our spillover variable,pi,last,ZIP0.5ml⁠, exhibit abnormal spatial clustering. This indicates that omitted, fixed geographical features (generically) are unlikely to drive a spurious relation with prices.The next test (Section 3.2) compares pricing spillovers involving housei’s very recent competitors (selling less than 30 days ago), and those from a few weeks prior (31-90 days ago). Here, the assumption is that neither a fixed nor an induced location feature should have changed meaningfully over such a short horizon. Hence, if we find that current and/or very recent comparable transactions have marginally bigger impact on the selling price of housei, price spillovers are more likely.The tests in Section 3.3 combine data on permitted renovations with the timing of historical sales. Controlling for renovations—both for houseiitself or its neighbors—has virtually no impact on the effect of comparable listings’ reference points on pricing.Our final test in Section 3.4 relies on the historical purchase timing of second homes. The identifying assumption is that second home purchases may establish reference points in the minds of sellers that affect pricing for primary homes. However, their non-local nature eliminates omitted heterogeneity in micro-location by construction.

3.1 Reference point clustering

In this section, we develop a test to directly characterize the within-ZIP code spatial distribution of houses surrounded by comparables with low reference points (levels of the ZIP code price index at time of purchase), compared to that of houses with comparables with higher reference points. Intuitively, if houses with low reference point comparables capture a within-ZIP code location disamenity (e.g., noise), they would be disproportionately clustered in the specific areas where this factor is prevalent.

ConsiderFigure 2, which reports the locations of house sales in Pasadena, California. The figure comprises four panels, which report sales taking place, respectively, in the first half of 2017, in the second half of 2017, in the first half of 2018, and in the second half of 2018. The different shades represent different ZIP codes. Within each ZIP code, we divide sales into two groups. The first (black diamonds) consists of houses that have relatively low reference points across comparables, defined as having a value of the spillover variable,plast,ZIP0.5ml⁠, below the median over the entire sample in the ZIP code. The second group (red dots) consists of the complementary set of houses, with high comparables reference points, which means having a value ofplast,ZIP0.5mlgreater or equal than the median. The figure anecdotally shows that,within each ZIP code and over time, houses with below median comparable reference points are not systematically more clustered, or in other words, closer together than other sales, and instead seem to have a similar distribution of occurrences in space.

To establish this more formally, we use the following approach. Within each ZIP code, we construct a “low comparables” reference points and “high comparables” reference points group, as described above. We then measure the average distance between each member of each group and all the other members of the same group. This gives us a sense of how clustered the members of each group are. Finally, we measure the average distance between each member of the “low comparables” group, and each member of the “high comparables” group. If low and high comparables group members were distributed identically in space, then each member of the low comparables group should be, on average, as close to the members of the high comparables group as it is to the members of its own group. On the other hand, if the members of the low comparables group are clustered, they will be on average closer to each other, and farther apart from the members of the high comparables group.

Table 4reports the statistics for the sales data. In columns 1 and 2 of panel A, we show the mean, median, and standard deviation of the average distance within groups across 943 ZIP codes in our sample. For members of the low comparables reference point groups, the mean distance across ZIP codes is 1.32 miles; the median is 1.23 miles; and the standard deviation across ZIP codes is 0.63. Statistics for the high comparables groups are virtually identical. In the table, we refer to the average within-group differences asμμ(dist,LL)andμμ(dist,HH)⁠.

Although the tests herein suggest that variation inpi,last,ZIP0.5mlis unlikely to be capturing unobserved heterogeneity in micro-location, we conduct a further test intended to mitigate this possibility by sample construction. Specifically, we drop observations in which the house whose price we are attempting to explain (i.e., housei) was bought at a similar historical index level as were neighboring listings. The goal is to ensure that there is “distance” between aggregate conditions when houseiwas purchased and when local neighbors acquired their houses. Hence, even if we had observed spatial clustering in the realizations ofpi,last,ZIP0.5ml⁠, estimation on this selected sample would eliminate such cases via brute force.Internet AppendixTable B.3shows the result of this exercise, in which we eliminate all observations where|log(pi,last,ZIP)−log(pi,last,ZIP0.5ml)|is in the bottom quartile.15In the restricted sample, for bothEquation (1)and(2), and in both the sales and rental market, our estimates remain largely unchanged.

3.2 Contemporaneous versus recent listings

A second test relies on the assumption that, on average, locational attributes are relatively long-lived. Despite some exceptions (e.g., the start of noisy construction), most value-relevant characteristics, either fixed or related to investments, are unlikely to change abruptly. Hence, if we are concerned thatpi,last,ZIP0.5mlis picking up durable aspects of intra-ZIP-code location, we can use thelaggedvalue ofpi,last,ZIP0.5mlas a placebo variable. An appropriately selected placebo period will capture the price impact of virtually all locational attributes, but yet be sufficiently lagged to eliminate most competitive dynamics involving housei. For this exercise, we select for the placebo period a horizon of 31-90 days, but have experimented with several alternative horizons, with almost no change in the results.

We reestimate the reduced-form specification inEquation (1), as well as the two-stage approach inEquation (2), but modified to capture the spillovers from the 31- to 90-day placebo comparables sample.Table 6reports the results. Columns 1 and 2 (reduced-form) and 3 and 4 (two-stage) show a near-zero impact for both placebo variables on prices. The nil effect of the placebo variables complements the results of the last section, and suggests that unobserved heterogeneity in location is unlikely as a plausible alternative.

3.3 Renovations

Whereas the two prior sections addressed potential correlation between historically derived reference points and micro-location, this short section explores the role played by property improvements. The specific concern is that ebbs and flows of the real estate cycle might correlate with renovations or other investments that improve a house’s value. For the own-effect coefficient, a bias might obtain if houses purchased when aggregate prices were high (e.g., 2004-2006) are more likely to have been renovated. For the spillover coefficient to be biased, an additional step is required. Here, the concern is that controlling for housei’s own historical timing and any improvements (which may bias the own-effect), renovations by housei’s competitors may have positive quality spillovers. To the extent that such renovations are related to original acquisition timing, a spurious relation may result.16Alternatively, a spurious relation can also arise if the fact that neighboring houses are more likely to be renovated, or to attain higher sales prices or rents, may in turn make the owner of houseimore likely to renovate her house.

To explore these alternative explanations, we obtain property-level data on remodeling permits from Corelogic, and merge these data with both sales and rental data by address. We then augment the specification ofEquation (1)using various specifications that address remodeling activity. We first construct variables capturing comparables remodeling activity: a dummy equal to one for houses with at least one comparable that was remodeled between purchase and resale (or purchase and rental listing), a dummy equal to one for houses with at least one comparable that was remodeled in the last 3 years before sale (or rental), the logarithm of (one plus) the average number of permits across comparable listings, and the same variable but restricted to the last 3 years. We then construct analogous variables for the remodeling activity of houseiitself, for example, a dummy equal to one for houses that were remodeled between purchase and resale (or purchase and rental listing), and so on.

Tables 7and8present the results for sales and rentals, respectively. All remodeling variables have positive coefficients, and many are statistically significant. This is consistent with the idea that both a house’s own renovations and those of its neighbors can affect its appeal to potential buyers. However, for our purposes, more important is that both coefficients for the own and spillover reference points are minimally affected, suggesting that renovations that coincide with the real estate cycle are unlikely to explain our findings.

3.4 Reference point spillovers between first and second homes

A final test in this section exploits the fact that in some cases, we observe the sale of homes that do not serve as primary residences. Although the set of such houses is much smaller than our overall sample (less than 1%), testing forintra-ownerreference point spillovers—whereby the historical purchase of a primary home influences the pricing strategies of a second or rental home—offers three identification advantages.

First, insofar as we are concerned that a second home’s own purchase price may reflect information about its (within-ZIP code) location or unobserved quality, the ability of a primary home’s purchase price to reflect either seems limited. Second, the nature of the test is within owners, and hence, severs any linkage between the aggregate real estate cycle and owner attributes, such as risk aversion, wealth, or discount rates.17Third, and finally, note that in addition to sellers,buyersof second/rental homes may also observe historical selling dates and prices—portals, such as Zillow and RedFin, make both easy to observe—and hence, may have valuations influenced by either. Focusing the analysis on a seller’s other properties (e.g., a primary residence) makes this considerably less likely.

To this end, we select in the data properties that are second homes, based on a mismatch between the residence address of the seller, and the property address (as inChinco and Mayer 2016). We then identify sellers’ main residence addresses that are residential, and find the historical purchase price at which sellers’ bought their main residence. In total, we find approximately 1,300 properties in our data set that are second homes, and for which we can track the historical purchase prices of the sellers’ primary residence. These purchase prices carry little to no information on the value of second homes, which have different characteristics and are located in different ZIP codes. However, they may create individual reference points for the sellers, and, as mentioned above, these historical purchase prices would be difficult to observe for buyers, and thus cannot influence their beliefs.

Table 9shows the result, which is that the historical purchase price of the seller’s main residence affects the resale price of their second home.18We reestimateEquation (1), but augmented with a dummy equal to one for properties identified as second homes, and an interaction term between the dummy and the logarithm of purchase price of the corresponding primary residence. This second term captures the elasticity of the second home resale price to the historical purchase price of the primary residence. We find that the relationship is significant, even after controlling for the second home’s own historical index level at the time of purchase. The implied elasticity is about 0.4%, which is about one-sixth of our benchmark effect. With the caveat that the small sample size results in an imprecise estimate, a natural interpretation is that while sellers appear to be influenced by reference points established by other properties in their portfolios, the most salient effect involves the history of the candidate property itself.

article.4 Price Competition: Model and Evidence:
So far, we have shown that a historically derived reference point involving one seller affects not only his own future selling behavior, but also that of competing sellers. Several pieces of evidence suggest that this pattern represents price spillovers, rather than omitted heterogeneity in house or location characteristics. What we have not explored, however, is the transmission mechanism; that is, why the extra-hedonic pricing residual for one seller appears to induce a strategic response by nearby competitors.

There are two main possibilities. First, sellers and/or buyers (or landlords and/or tenants) could be extracting information perceived as relevant for a given house from the prices of competing houses. Provided that there is a common value component to real estate (e.g., location), but one that is not perfectly observed by all parties, prices may contain information about each seller’s private signal. In this case, we might expect for price residuals to be correlated between neighboring houses. A second alternative obtains from simple price (i.e., Bertrand) competition. In this framework, sellers (or landlords) compete with each other, and with any potential buyers (or tenants), over surplus. Holding house quality constant, a seller who raises (lowers) her price reduces (increases) the competitiveness of the market, causing other sellers to increase (decrease) their prices accordingly.

It is worth noting that the first possibility, whereby sellers and/or buyers (or landlords and/or tenants) are using prices to back out the private information of competitors, does not correspond to signal extraction as commonly understood. The reason is because our empirical analysis focuses on the part of the pricing residual explained by the seller’s historical purchase timing which, as shown in Section 3, does not appear plausibly related to house or location characteristics. Hence, to the extent that buyers or competing sellers attempt to infer information from theseuninformativeprice residuals, any updating leads to less, not more, efficient pricing. Of course, it is possible that sellers and/or buyers may not distinguish between a generic price residual and its projection onto historical timing or purchase price, despite both being publicly observable. We therefore take this possibility seriously, despite its theoretical challenges.

The analysis in this section is organized into two parts. The first presents a stylized model based onLazear (1986), but we apply the model to a real estate market involving multiple sellers (or landlords). The model shows how imperfect competition can generate price spillovers. How the model applies to the rental market is described in Section 4.4. Two empirical predictions emerge from this exercise. First, a more competitive framework should reduce the spillover effect. Second, for a given level of competition, a seller’s strategic response alters its selling probabilities (e.g., responding to an overpriced house results in an elevated price, but nevertheless, higher chance of selling). With these predictions established, we return to the data in the second part of this section, and find support for both.

4.1 One seller

Consider a single seller whose house has value v ∼ U [ 0 , 1 ] ⁠ . The seller, knowing only the distribution of v , but not its realization, posts a take-it-or-leave-it price R , for which the buyer can purchase the house ( ⁠ v ≥ R ) ⁠ , or if not ( ⁠ v < R ) ⁠ , search for another one. There is a single period, all parties are risk-neutral, and the reservation utility for the owner and the buyer are both zero. The owner’s problem is max R R ( 1 − F [ R ] ) = max R R ( 1 − R ) , (3) where F [ . ] is the cdf of the uniform distribution. The optimal rent is R * = 1 2 ⁠ , and expected revenue is 1 4 ⁠ . The owner’s ex ante lack of information about v means that she cannot ask a higher (lower) price for a house of higher (lower) quality, and so trades off the probability of selling ( ⁠ 1 − R ⁠ ) against the price conditional on a successful match ( R ). In this specific case, the expected surplus from trade is 1 4 + ∫ R * 1 ( v − R * ) d v = 3 8 < E [ v ] = 1 2 ⁠ . The efficiency loss obtains from situations in which R > v , and the house does not sell. 19

4.2 One seller: Extra-hedonic bias induced by reference points

Now, consider an additional piece of information which is uninformative about the distribution ofv, but which nevertheless influences the seller’s pricing decisions. For example, imagine a liquidity-constrained seller wishing to purchase another house, but without sufficient cash on hand for a down payment. In this case, the seller’s trade-off would depart from the benchmark case above and would take into account the sales priceRrelative to a reference pointC, perhaps the existing mortgage balance and any selling commissions. Another possibility is loss aversion, which incorporates reference points directly into the utility function itself. Here, the seller would experience a utility loss if the sales priceRfails to exceed a reference point, often the original sales price. A final possibility might be a seller who is biased about the house’s value through, for example, historical experiences that the seller (incorrectly) views as being informative about fundamental value,v.

For the purposes of motivating our empirical analysis, it is less critical which of these mechanisms creates the price distortion. The reason is that we are interested in the strategic responses of competing sellers, whose pricing decisions are assumed tonotthemselves directly depend on reference points. Rather, because transmission of one seller’s reference point to others is mediated solely through prices, the pricing decisions of competing sellers effectively take any nonbenchmark pricing by the original seller as given. Hence, we introduce price distortions using a particularly simple framework, whereby a biased seller is simply endowed with a valuation error.

Specifically, we propose that the seller’s subjective valuation diverges from the fair (or the buyers’) valuation for the house:v∼U[Δ,1+Δ]⁠, whereΔ∈{−X,X}is the extra-hedonic component, with0≤X≤12.To connect this assumption with the evidence discussed in the previous sections, consider Δ as a reduced-form representation of the effects of reference points (themselves established by purchase histories) on the owner’s valuation. The first case,Δ=−X⁠, corresponds to a low reference point, whereas the second case,Δ=X⁠, implies an owner who has a high reference point.20The owner will setR*=1+2Δ2⁠, which is either too high, or too low, byX, with respect to the price set by an unbiased owner.21

4.3 Two competing sellers

We now consider the implications for pricing in the presence of multiple owners. As with the one-house case above, we start with a benchmark case with no reference points. Multiple owners compete, and accordingly, prices drop relative to the monopolistic case. We then consider the situation that arises when one owner is biased, due to the presence of a reference point, about his own house value, but the other one is not. As in the one-house case, the biased owner ends up distorting the price asked on his house, but through competitive effects, so too does the unbiased owner.

Both sellers are unbiased. There is a single potential buyer with reservation utility zero, choosing between the two houses. The value to the buyer of either house i = 1, 2, v i , is independently and uniformly distributed on [ 0 , 1 ] ⁠ . Each owner i posts a take-it-or-leave-it price, R i , against which the buyer can compare his utility from buying to the available alternatives: buying the other house (– i ) or not buying either house. The condition for the buyer to purchase from owner 2 is that the surplus she attains from the purchase is positive, and greater than the one she would receive purchasing from owner 1: v 2 − R 2 ≥ v 1 − R 1 | v 2 − R 2 ≥ 0 ⁠ . It is important to highlight that the competition mechanism relies on the fact that sellers (or landlords) compete for buyers (tenants) and that there are gains from trade. In the framework, this happens because the reservation value of the seller, or landlord if we apply the model to the rental market, is equal to zero. However, this is just a normalization, all we need is that buyers and tenants value houses more than sellers and landlords.

4.4 Applying the model to the rental market

The model described above presents a useful benchmark for both the sales and rental markets, capturing the idea that owner 1’s price setting behavior is affected by an extra-hedonic element, which biases his perception of the consumption flow generated by the property. This influences his views on the rent that can be extracted from the property, and hence the property price, to the extent that this is equivalent to the present value of future rental flows. If so, thenvis the capitalized flow of consumption in the sales market,v=vrental/c⁠, wherecis the local market capitalization rate. It follows directly that ifcis approximately constant, the model applies equally well to the rental or sales market.

We may then wonder whether the mechanism that causes owner 1’s historical conditions to affect current prices and/or rents, itself, depends on capitalization rates. While we do not see a clear connection between the hedonic bias and current cap rates, it seems plausible that time-series variation in the cap rate could be, at least in the case of the distorted beliefs explanation of the model, driving the effect.

Consider a house that, between two points in time 0 and 1, has not changed, so that the rental flow value isvrental,0≈vrental,1⁠, that is, both unobservable and observable qualities are constant. Let us further assume, for simplicity, that the growth rate of rental flows is small, and that we can evaluate housing consumption flows as perpetuities. If we denote the current timet= 1, and the date of the most recent sale ast= 0, thenv1≈v0((r0−g0)/(r1−g1))⁠. If property valuations have fallen via increases inr, then the quantity in parentheses is less than one, and vice versa.

The seller’s and landlord’s problems are to estimatev1andvrental,1⁠, or as in the model, the distribution describing them. He observes the lagged purchase pricePi,0⁠, but must fully account for the changes in the cap rate, due to changes in discount rates, since the last sale. Otherwise, part of the historical price will be misattributed to unobservable quality features, and to the extent that these are persistent, will cause the landlord to set a rent which is too high, or too low. Does this mechanism describe, at least in part, landlords’ and sellers’ thinking process? The problem of conflating average house quality and cap rates is an important measurement issue, to which real estate index providers (e.g. Zillow, S&P Case-Shiller) allocate substantial effort. Hence, it seems reasonable that a landlord or seller, even with help from a real estate professional, might fail to perfectly make this distinction.

One piece of evidence, presented byGiacoletti and Parsons (2022), that seems to support anchoring to historical conditions, in the way described, is found when restricting the analysis to landlords who have little, or no, debt associated with their purchases. This minimizes the importance of the liquidity constraints channel, as well as any prospect theory considerations (e.g., a landlord setting rent to cover a monthly payment net of all expenses). Given that rents in California are so high, a landlord with minimal debt is almost certainly cash flow positive every month, so the kink in gains/losses around this particular reference point would not seem relevant. And still, among this group—for which distorted beliefs are the most likely candidate—historical, index-level, prices influence rent-setting today.

4.5 Empirical predictions

The model generates three empirical predictions. First, higher competition reduces prices. We can see that in the transition from the single unbiased seller, to the two unbiased sellers framework. The house price drops from 0.5 to2−1⁠. This is consistent with the evidence inTables 2and3, which show that the number of nearby comparable listings has a negative effect on sales prices and rents. This also validates the number of comparables as a proxy for competition.

Second, the spillover of reference points relies critically on imperfect competition. Intuitively, with perfect competition and atomistic sellers, distorted decisions of individual sellers will not affect the behavior of their competitors. Likewise, with fewer competitors, prices are both higher and more sensitive to price changes of competitors. This generates a testable prediction, which we can explore in the data using interactions between the degree of local competition and the average price level experienced by competitors at the time of purchase. The coefficient for the interaction should be negative, reflecting the idea that a more competitive selling environment mitigates each seller’s sensitivity to the price changes of others. Importantly, this second test also can be used to distinguish our conjectured mechanism from the alternative of prices being used for signal extraction, either by sellers or by buyers. If either party uses historical information to form their beliefs, one would expect that the spillover effects would becomestrongerwith a larger number of nearby competitors, which presumably conveys a more precise signal. Under this alternative theory, the sign of the interaction coefficient should be positive.22

Third, the model has predictions about sales probabilities, which allow us to further distinguish between a mechanism based on competition versus signal extraction. Panel B ofFigure 4shows the sales probabilities for the biased and unbiased sellers, in the two-sellers model. For the biased seller, the probability of sale is negatively correlated with his own bias. This is because when his bias is negative, he offers a larger surplus to the buyer than in the unbiased case, and the opposite is true when the bias is positive. However, note that the spillover effect on sales probabilities for the unbiased seller is positively correlated with the bias. The reason is that the unbiased seller receiving the spillover is only partially adjusting his price in response to his competitor’s bias, which makes him less likely to sell when the bias is negative (his sales price is higher than his competitor’s), and more likely to sell when the bias is positive (his sales price is lower than his competitor’s).

If, instead, the spillover effects were driven by biases in beliefs, there would be no effect on sales probabilities. Buyers would shift their views on the fair market value of both houses. Likewise, sellers would adjust their prices to incorporate these updated views, up to the point when they both offer the same surplus to the buyers, and the probability of sale for the two properties would thus be uncorrelated with the sign or magnitude of the bias.

4.6.2 Selling probabilities

To construct tests based on sales probabilities and time on the market, we obtain Multilple Listing Serivices (MLS) micro-data for California from Corelogic, providing information on individual single family residences listed for sale in 2017 and 2018. For each listing, we calculate the logarithm of the average ZIP code index at time of purchase for comparables purchased in the 30 days following the original listing date. We then construct dummies equal to one for listings that sold within 30, 60, 45 and 90 days after the original listing date. We estimate logit regressions to measure the effect of the logarithm of the ZIP code index across comparable listings on sales probabilities, after controlling for house characteristics (dummies for number of bedrooms, bathrooms, logarithm of size, age, and age squared), ZIP code fixed effects and monthly seasonal fixed effects.

Table 11reports are our results. We find that the logarithm of the average ZIP code index across comparables has a positive and significant effect on the probability of sale across all four horizons. In relative terms, a 50% higher index at time of purchase across competitors translates into a 3%–4% relatively higher probability of sale within each horizon. This is consistent with the predictions of the competition mechanism. The higher the reference points, the lower is the surplus offered by biased sellers to buyers; hence, high reference points across comparables induce positive spillovers for both prices and sales probabilities for neighbors. On the other hand, our findings are not consistent with the predictions of a model in which buyers make biased inferences based on comparable sales, which does not predict spillovers on sales probabilities or time on the market.

article.5 Aggregate (ZIP-Code-Level) Effects:
We conclude by zooming out from the micro-analysis, and exploring whether, when aggregated, the spillover and direct effects of reference points explain differences in house prices and rents across California ZIP codes. To this end, we develop a bottom-up approach, which relies on our transaction-level estimates from the hedonic model inEquation (1), along with the distribution of historical purchase timings and local price indexes across ZIP codes.

In particular, for each ZIP code we calculate the average direct and indirect effect of reference dependence on the resale price of individual properties, using the equations below: Δ p direct , z = 1 N z ∑ i = 1 N z δ ^ ( log ( p i , last , z ) − log ( p i , z ) ) (10) Δ p spill , z = 1 N z , 0.5 m l ∑ i = 1 N z , 0.5 m l γ ^ ( log ( p i , last , z 0.5 m l ) − log ( p i , z ) ) (11) Δ p tot , z = Δ p direct , z + Δ p spill , z (12) where δ ^ and γ ^ are the estimates of the corresponding coefficients from the hedonic model in Equation (1) , log ( p i , z ) is the logarithm of the ZIP code house price index in the month of resale i , log ( p i , last , z ) is the logarithm of the index level at the time of purchase of i , log ( p i , last , z 0.5 m l ) is the logarithm of the average level of the index at the time of purchase of comparables within 0.5 miles, N z is the number of resales in postcode z , and N z , 0.5 m l is the number of resales in postcode z with at least a comparable within 0.5 miles.

Thus,Δpdirect,zcaptures the average effect of reference dependence, based on the own-historical level of prices at the time of purchase. Note that when the current house price index is the same as the historical level at time of purchase, reference dependence has no effect on the current resale price. Since the estimateδ^is positive, if the house was purchased at a time when the index was below the current level, the effect of reference dependence on the resale price is negative, and vice versa if the house was purchased at a time when price levels were higher.Δpspill,zcaptures the indirect, spillover, effect that has been the focus of the previous sections. In this case, since the estimateγ^is positive, if the average price level experienced by competitors at the time of purchase is higher than the current level, the effect is positive. Finally,Δptot,zrepresents the sum of the average direct and spillover effects from reference dependence in ZIP codez.

Figure 5shows histograms that summarize the distributions ofΔpdirect,z,Δpspill,z⁠, andΔptot,zin the cross-section of California ZIP codes. The direct effect is on average equal to –0.76%, which is consistent with the observation that California prices were close to an all-time high in 2017 and 2018, and most sellers had below-market reference points. However, even though almost all ZIP codes experience a negative reference point effect, the estimated magnitudes are dispersed across ZIP codes, with an interquartile range between –1% and –0.25%. The average spillover effect is –0.25%, equal to roughly one-third of the direct effect, and with substantial dispersion across ZIP codes. The combined effect, on average, is -1%, with an interquartile range between –1.4% and –0.4%. Thus, roughly a quarter of the combined effect is driven by spillovers, which makes them quantitatively important even at this aggregate level.

article.6 Conclusions:
Existing research mainly focuses on the direct effects of biases, cognitive errors, and frictions on individual decision-making. For instance, the historical price level at the time of a house purchase generates a reference point, which affects resales prices and the rents asked by landlords. However, when biased agents have market power, their actions may distort the behavior of other unbiased agents through competitive pressure, thus generating contagion, or spillovers, of biases.

Using data from the house sales and rentals market in California, we show that historical conditions matter not only directly, but also through one’s competitors: 50% of the direct effect of historical price levels on the current resales price or asked rent of nearby competitors, located within half-mile, spills over onto the price or rent set for a specific property. We conduct a variety of tests to show that these effects are not driven by unobservable elements of housing or micro-location quality, correlated with the timing of comparables’ purchases.

We then show in a simple model that the spillovers can be explained through limited competition in housing markets, and construct empirical tests providing further evidence that imperfect competition is the most likely channel through which reference points spillovers are propagated.

Finally, we construct, at the ZIP code level, the aggregate sum of the combined direct and spillover effect of prices at the time of purchase on current resales and rentals. We find that these effects can determine substantial wedges in prices and rents across ZIP codes, and that between a quarter and a third of the wedges are driven by the spillover component.

We view our paper as a potential step toward a more complete understanding of how reference points, even established several years ago, continue to affect real estate markets, through both direct and spillover effects. One hope with this paper is to draw attention to the role imperfect competition may play in propagating pricing errors from one asset (or seller) to another. Whereas this consideration will be less important for assets with flatter demand curves, such as stocks, the theoretical arguments and evidence presented here may be relevant not only to real estate but also to other illiquid assets with more elastic demand curves.

13 We calculate standard errors using a two-step simulation algorithm, to account for measurement error in e ^ i , last , ZIP 0.5 m l ⁠ . First, we simulate vectors e j , b = e ^ j + u 2 , j , b − u 1 , j , b ⁠ , where b = 1 , 2 , … , 100 and u 2 , j , b , u 1 , j , b are i.i.d. normal with mean zero and variance equal to the variance of residuals from the corresponding hedonic regressions. We then construct e ^ i , b , last , ZIP 0.5 m l for each draw e j , b ⁠ , and estimate Equation (2) . For each estimate (each b draw), we then run a second simulation, in which we simulate 10,000 values of the regression parameters. Finally, we calculate the standard error as the standard deviation of coefficients across the 100 × 10 , 000 = 1 , 000 , 000 simulations.

14 For the low groups, the average and median distances are 1.3 and 1.18 miles, across 671 ZIP codes in our sample. The mean and median of the average distance between the members of the low and high groups across ZIP codes are 1.41 and 1.27 miles. When we measure the difference in the average distances across ZIP codes, we find that the mean is 0.11 miles; the median is 0.06; and the standard deviation is 0.37. When looking at the distribution of the difference in distances across ZIP codes, the average difference is larger than 0.25 miles in 20% of ZIP codes, but larger than 0.5 miles only in 7% of them, and larger than 1 mile in 3%. At the 95% level, we can reject the null that the distance is larger than 0.1 miles in 17% of ZIP codes, larger than 0.25 miles in 9% of ZIP codes, larger than 0.5 miles in 4% of ZIP codes, and larger than 1 mile in 1%.

18 Note that for this analysis, we are using the actual transaction prices of primary homes, rather than the ZIP-code-level index. Our other tests use index-level historical pricing to mitigate unobserved heterogeneity in house quality, but here, the comparison is not only between different houses but also usually quite far apart. In our sample, none are located in the same ZIP code as the primary residence. In this case, we use historical transactions prices themselves, as in Genesove and Mayer (2001), which exhibit considerably more variation compared to ZIP code indexes, partly offsetting the disadvantage in statistical power created by focusing on a small set of second homes.

19 In our framework, we can introduce any periodic cost or payment that does not depend on R without affecting the outcome of the optimization problem and the choice of R * = 1 2 ⁠ . This captures the intuition that property taxes, mortgage payments, and other periodic expenses that the owner has to honor on its property should not affect the choice of the optimal price. One limitation of our approach is that the demand curve takes a particularly simple form, in which the probability a house sells or rents is linear in price or rent. More sophisticated frameworks (e.g., Genesove and Han 2012; Head, Huw, and Sun 2014; Guren 2018) indicate that this may not be realistic. However, this simplification does not change the key model predictions that are of interest to our analysis.

20 Note that Δ can be considered a single-parameter stand in for the mispricing that would obtain from an anchoring to historical conditions. For instance, suppose that a seller obtains an unbiased signal of value, ϵ s = v + ϵ with ϵ E ( ϵ ) = 0 ⁠ . Hence, an unbiased seller will ask R * = E ( v ) = s which, given the uniform distribution of v , will be 1 2 ⁠ . A biased seller who places weight δ on an irrelevant anchor A believes that δ δ E ( v ) = δ A + ( 1 − δ ) s ⁠ . This seller will set a price of δ R * = 1 2 + δ 2 ( 2 A − 1 ) ⁠ . Δ summarizes both the realization of the anchor ( A ) and the seller’s weighting of it ( δ ).

21 An alternative might involve modifying the objective function to ( 1 − R ) ( R + g ( X , R ) ) ⁠ , where g ( X , R ) captures the effect of an additional utility or monetary flow depending on the realization of X and R . In this case, the first-order condition (FOC) with respect to R is [ 1 + g ′ ( X , R * ) ] ( 1 − R * ) = [ R * + g ( X , R * ) ] ⁠ . Taking the total derivative and collecting terms yields ∂ R * ∂ x = ( 1 − R * ) g X R ( X , R * ) − g X ( X , R * ) 2 + 2 g R ( X , R * ) + ( R * − 1 ) g R R ( X , R * ) . That this expression depends on g X R ( X , R * ) and g R R ( X , R * ) means that more economic structure is required to determine the algebraic sign. For example, if g ( · ) represents prospect theory preferences, the function will be convex in the loss region, and concave in the domain of gains. Although Giacoletti and Parsons (2022) argue that in the specific market under consideration (California circa 2018-2019), almost all owners and landlords could expect a gain from transacting, the resultant parametric restrictions they employ are empirically rather than theoretically motivated. These assumptions may not hold in other markets with different temporal dynamics, meaning that an unambiguous relation between R * and X does not necessarily follow under a generic formulation for g ( · ) ⁠ . The stylized model we present should therefore be understood as serving an illustrative purpose, with the primary focus of the paper on the empirical patterns.

22 It is important to note that for the buyers’ beliefs channel to explain spillovers, the average historical price of comparables, and not just the historical purchase price of each individual house, must influences buyers’ views. In fact, if buyers’ views are just affected by individual house purchase prices, reference points spillovers do not arise. This is because buyers think that a house purchased at a time when the price level was different than today (a “biased” house) is different than a house purchased at a price level similar to the one today (an “unbiased” house). Houses will sell at different prices but offer the same surplus to buyers, and thus there would be no spillover.
